text: The literature has been debating the existence of truly cross-pressured citizens who hold a mix of liberal and conservative policy views. Some recent work suggests that such citizens are actually centrist, such as the work by Fowler et al. ( 2023) who use a mixture model to argue that nearly all mixed agenda voters are "Downsian" ideologues whose preferences are well-explained by a single-dimensional model and that those with moderate estimated scores are centrist. We show that the analysis in Fowler et al. exaggerates the homogeneity of the Conversational category. First, we observe that the modelling of this category as a single homogeneous cluster is not justifiable given the rich description of non-ideological belief systems that are depicted in the classical public opinion literature. Second, using Monte Carlo simulations of the mixture model and reanalysing the data in Fowler et al., we document cases in which the model assigns large numbers of truly cross-pressured and non-ideological individuals to the moderate ideologue category, not because of any difference in their policy preferences but because a line is a more flexible geometric form than a point. Hence, the claim that moderate individuals have centrist preferences remains unproven. We conclude by arguing that it will be necessary to have more refined types of data—that is, richer ordinal and panel data—that allow for more nuanced characterizations of citizens' preferences and thereby allow for discrimination between idiosyncratic and centrist preferences.
